past_medical_history: Generalized anxiety disorder, diagnosed 5 years ago
Depression, diagnosed 3 years ago, currently in partial remission
One prior emergency department visit 18 months ago for chest pain, cardiac workup negative

medications.frequency: as needed, up to three times daily

family_history: Mother has fibromyalgia and generalized anxiety disorder
Father has hypertension and type 2 diabetes
No family history of stroke or multiple sclerosis

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine on weekends
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her partner in an apartment
social_history.recent_stressors: Reports significant work stress related to a contentious relationship with a new school principal. Her partner recently lost his job two months ago. She was also involved in a minor rear-end motor vehicle accident 6 weeks ago with no significant injury but found it very distressing.
social_history.exercise: Previously active with regular yoga and running, stopped 4 weeks ago due to leg weakness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech fluent with normal rate, rhythm, and prosody. No dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact, able to spell WORLD backwards and perform serial sevens without error. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. Affect is somewhat flat but cooperative. She appears anxious and focused on her leg symptoms.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam normal, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus or diplopia. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions bilaterally. Masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag reflex intact. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline, no fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in all extremities, no spasticity or rigidity. Upper extremities: strength 5/5 bilaterally in all muscle groups (deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip). Right lower extremity: give-way weakness in hip flexion, knee extension, and ankle dorsiflexion — patient initiates movement to 4/5 then suddenly gives way. Left lower extremity: 5/5 in all muscle groups. Hoover sign POSITIVE on the right — involuntary hip extension detected when testing contralateral hip flexion, contradicting apparent voluntary weakness. No pronator drift. No muscle atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the entire right leg in a non-dermatomal distribution with a sharp midline split at the groin. Vibration and proprioception intact throughout. Sensory findings do not conform to any peripheral nerve, root, or spinal cord pattern.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. Reflexes are symmetric and normal, inconsistent with upper or lower motor neuron lesion.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing normal bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing normal on the left. On the right, patient demonstrates inconsistent performance with moments of normal coordination interspersed with apparent difficulty. No intention tremor. Rapid alternating movements intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Patient initially states she cannot walk. When assisted to standing, demonstrates a dragging gait of the right leg with inconsistent pattern. When distracted by conversation, leg movements briefly normalize. No Trendelenburg sign. Tandem gait not attempted due to patient's reported inability. Romberg testing: dramatic swaying without falling, with ability to self-correct. Overall gait pattern is non-physiologic.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, the patient had a witnessed episode of bilateral upper and lower extremity shaking lasting approximately 90 seconds. Eyes were noted to be forcefully closed during the event. The shaking was arrhythmic, waxing and waning, with side-to-side head movements. She remained responsive to voice during portions of the event. No post-ictal confusion, no cyanosis, no tongue biting, no incontinence. The episode self-resolved with verbal reassurance. This is consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES). Video-EEG was being recorded during this event.

patient.chief_complaint: Right leg weakness and episodes of shaking spells for 4 weeks
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Lauren Hadley is a 28-year-old right-handed woman who presents to the neurology inpatient service with a four-week history of progressive right leg weakness and recurrent episodes of whole-body shaking. The symptoms began approximately four weeks ago when she noticed her right leg 'giving out' while walking up stairs at work. Over the following week the weakness progressed to the point where she had difficulty walking without a limp, and she has been using a cane for the past two weeks. She reports the leg feels 'dead' and she cannot control it. She also describes decreased sensation over the entire right leg, stating it feels numb from the hip down. Concurrently, she began experiencing episodes of shaking approximately three weeks ago. These episodes involve bilateral arm and leg trembling, sometimes with her eyes rolling back. They last between one and three minutes. During some episodes she reports being able to hear people around her but is unable to respond. There is no tongue biting, urinary incontinence, or post-ictal confusion. The episodes occur one to two times per day and seem to be triggered by emotional stress or fatigue. She was seen at an outside emergency department two weeks ago where a CT head was normal and she was referred for outpatient neurology follow-up, but her symptoms worsened and she was admitted for further evaluation including video-EEG monitoring. She reports significant psychosocial stress including workplace conflict with a new supervisor, her partner's recent unemployment, and a minor motor vehicle accident six weeks ago that she found very distressing despite no physical injury. She denies any prior history of seizures, stroke, or neurological disease. She has been taking sertraline for anxiety and depression. She is very concerned she may have multiple sclerosis or a brain tumor and has been researching her symptoms extensively online.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz symmetric alpha rhythm, well-modulated and reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_architecture: Normal sleep transients including vertex waves and sleep spindles observed during drowsiness and stage II sleep

findings.clinical_correlation: A clinical event consisting of 90 seconds of bilateral extremity shaking with forced eye closure was captured on video. The EEG during this event showed normal awake background activity throughout, with prominent muscle artifact. No epileptiform discharges, rhythmic evolution, or post-ictal suppression were observed. This dissociation between clinical manifestation and electrographic activity is consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES).

artifacts.note: Present during the clinical event, underlying cerebral activity discernible and normal

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes, no abnormal response, did not provoke clinical event
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response, no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: NORMAL EEG. A clinical seizure-like event was captured on video-EEG. The EEG remained entirely normal during the clinical episode, with no ictal or post-ictal electrographic changes. This finding is diagnostic of a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES). No interictal epileptiform discharges were seen. Background activity is normal and symmetric.
recommended_actions: Communicate video-EEG findings to patient and family in a supportive manner
Referral to psychiatry or psychologist experienced in functional neurological disorder

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No white matter signal abnormalities. No periventricular, juxtacortical, or infratentorial lesions
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion to suggest acute ischemia

additional_observations: Ventricles are normal in size and configuration
Basal ganglia, thalami, and brainstem are normal
Pituitary gland is normal in size
Major intracranial vessels demonstrate normal flow voids
mri.impression: NORMAL MRI of the brain with and without contrast. No evidence of demyelinating disease, acute or chronic infarction, mass lesion, or other structural abnormality to explain the patient's symptoms.
recommended_actions: No follow-up imaging required based on current findings

Special.test: Prolactin (post-event, drawn 20 minutes after clinical episode)
Special.clinical_significance: Normal post-event prolactin. In generalized tonic-clonic seizures, prolactin typically rises 2-3x baseline within 10-20 minutes. Normal level supports non-epileptic etiology of the captured event.

labs.interpretation: All laboratory values are within normal limits. Complete blood count, basic metabolic panel, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, vitamin B12, folate, and ANA are normal. Post-event prolactin drawn 20 minutes after a witnessed clinical episode is NOT elevated, supporting a non-epileptic etiology for the shaking event.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz symmetric alpha rhythm, well-formed and reactive

findings.clinical_correlation: Over 48 hours of continuous video-EEG monitoring, three habitual clinical events were captured. All events consisted of bilateral extremity shaking with forced eye closure, arrhythmic movements, waxing and waning intensity, and side-to-side head movements. Duration ranged from 45 seconds to 2 minutes 15 seconds. The patient was partially responsive during two of the three events. In all three events, the EEG showed normal awake background with no epileptiform correlate, no rhythmic evolution, and no post-ictal changes. These findings confirm the diagnosis of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES).

artifacts.note: Present during clinical events but underlying cerebral activity remained discernible and normal

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving, no photoparoxysmal response
activating_procedures.sleep_deprivation: Sleep recorded, normal sleep architecture, no epileptiform discharges in sleep

output.impression: NORMAL prolonged video-EEG monitoring over 48 hours. Three habitual clinical events were captured, all without any EEG correlate. This is diagnostic of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES). No interictal epileptiform discharges were identified. This study provides definitive evidence against epileptic seizures.
recommended_actions: Discuss PNES diagnosis with patient using supportive, non-dismissive approach
Referral to psychotherapy (CBT is evidence-based for PNES)
Consider referral to FND-specialized multidisciplinary program

Sensory_NCS.test: Right superficial peroneal nerve - SNAP amplitude

output.interpretation: NORMAL electrodiagnostic study. Motor and sensory nerve conduction studies of the right lower extremity are normal, with no evidence of peripheral neuropathy, plexopathy, or radiculopathy. Needle EMG of right lower extremity muscles and lumbar paraspinals shows no denervation, no myopathic changes, and normal recruitment. These findings rule out a neuromuscular cause of the patient's right leg weakness.

signal_characteristics.T2: Normal spinal cord signal throughout cervical and thoracic segments. No intramedullary signal abnormality.
signal_characteristics.T1: Normal spinal cord caliber and signal
signal_characteristics.Post_contrast: No abnormal enhancement of the spinal cord or meninges

additional_observations: Cervical vertebral body heights and alignment are normal
No significant disc herniations or spinal canal stenosis
Conus medullaris terminates at L1 level, normal position
output.impression: NORMAL MRI of the cervical and thoracic spine with and without contrast. No evidence of myelopathy, demyelinating lesions, cord compression, or structural abnormality. No findings to explain the patient's lower extremity weakness.
recommended_actions: Consider functional etiology given normal structural imaging

Cognitive_Assessment.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Total

Psychological_Measures.clinical_significance: Above threshold for probable PTSD, warrants further evaluation

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological testing reveals intact cognitive function across all domains tested, including attention, processing speed, memory, executive function, and visuospatial skills. Effort measures are adequate, indicating valid results. Psychological measures reveal moderate depression (PHQ-9 = 14), severe anxiety (GAD-7 = 16), and a PCL-5 score above the threshold for probable PTSD (38/80), likely related to the motor vehicle accident and cumulative psychosocial stressors. These psychological comorbidities are commonly associated with functional neurological disorder and are important treatment targets.
abnormal_values_summary: WAIS-IV Full Scale IQ: 116 standard score - Average to high-average intellectual functioning
PCL-5 (PTSD Screen): 40  - Above threshold for probable PTSD, warrants further evaluation

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder (Conversion Disorder) F44.4
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: With weakness and with attacks or seizures

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Major Depressive Disorder, recurrent, moderate F33.1
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Current episode in context of psychosocial stressors
Subthreshold symptoms related to MVA, warrants monitoring

output.interpretation: Psychiatric evaluation reveals a 28-year-old woman with functional neurological symptom disorder in the context of significant psychosocial stressors, comorbid generalized anxiety disorder, and moderate recurrent major depressive disorder. She reports a distressing motor vehicle accident 6 weeks prior to symptom onset and escalating occupational and financial stress. No history of childhood abuse disclosed, though patient acknowledges difficulty discussing her childhood in detail and is open to further exploration. She has subthreshold PTSD symptoms. No suicidal ideation. She is motivated for treatment and has good insight once the FND diagnosis was explained. Recommendations include optimizing antidepressant therapy (consider increasing sertraline or switching to an SNRI), cognitive behavioral therapy with a therapist experienced in FND, physiotherapy with a functional rehabilitation approach, and psychoeducation about FND. Prognosis is favorable given her young age, short symptom duration, and treatment motivation.
abnormal_values_summary: DSM-5 Diagnosis 1: Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder (Conversion Disorder) F44.4  - With weakness and with attacks or seizures
DSM-5 Diagnosis 2: Generalized Anxiety Disorder F41.1  - Chronic, partially treated
DSM-5 Diagnosis 3: Major Depressive Disorder, recurrent, moderate F33.1  - Current episode in context of psychosocial stressors
DSM-5 Rule-out: Post-traumatic Stress Disorder F43.10  - Subthreshold symptoms related to MVA, warrants monitoring

output.query: functional neurological disorder treatment evidence-based therapy

results.title: Cognitive behavioural therapy for functional neurological symptoms: a randomised controlled trial (CODES trial)
results.authors: Goldstein LH, Robinson EJ, Mellers JDC, et al.
results.key_finding: CBT specifically designed for dissociative seizures reduced seizure frequency compared to standard medical care at 12 months

results.title: Physiotherapy for functional motor disorders: a consensus recommendation
results.authors: Nielsen G, Stone J, Matthews A, et al.
results.key_finding: Specialized physiotherapy using movement retraining, distraction techniques, and education is effective for functional motor symptoms

results.title: Functional neurological disorder: new subtypes and shared mechanisms
results.authors: Espay AJ, Aybek S, Carson A, et al.
results.key_finding: FND is best diagnosed by positive clinical signs (e.g., Hoover sign) rather than by exclusion. Multidisciplinary treatment is recommended.

output.summary: Current evidence supports a multidisciplinary approach to FND treatment. The CODES trial demonstrated that CBT reduces dissociative seizure frequency. Specialized physiotherapy using distraction-based movement retraining is effective for functional motor symptoms. Diagnosis should be made by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, inconsistency, incongruity) rather than exclusion alone. Early diagnosis and explanation improve prognosis. Antidepressants may help comorbid mood/anxiety disorders but do not directly treat FND.

warnings: Monitor for serotonin syndrome symptoms if combining with other serotonergic agents
May lower seizure threshold at higher doses, though this patient's events are non-epileptic
alternatives: Venlafaxine 75-150 mg daily (SNRI - may be considered if inadequate response to SSRI)
Duloxetine 60 mg daily (SNRI - dual benefit for mood and any comorbid pain)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with weakness and psychogenic non-epileptic seizures

differential.key_features: Shaking episodes lack EEG correlate, arrhythmic movements, forced eye closure, retained awareness, no post-ictal confusion, normal post-event prolactin — all inconsistent with epileptic seizures
Normal brain and spine MRI with no white matter lesions, normal inflammatory markers, normal neurological exam apart from functional signs, sensory findings in non-dermatomal pattern
Young patient with no vascular risk factors, normal MRI with no DWI restriction, weakness does not follow vascular territory, positive functional signs on exam
Normal reflexes, normal EMG/NCS, sensory loss in non-dermatomal distribution, give-way weakness pattern
No fatigable weakness, no ptosis or diplopia, no bulbar symptoms, weakness pattern inconsistent with neuromuscular junction disorder

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination looking for positive functional signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, inconsistency between formal testing and observed function, non-dermatomal sensory loss)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hoover sign positive, give-way weakness, midline splitting of sensory loss, inconsistent gait pattern

optimal_actions.action: Order video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual events and correlate with electrographic activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical events captured without EEG correlate, confirming PNES

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to exclude structural pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI, no demyelination, no mass, no infarction

optimal_actions.action: Order basic laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, and post-event prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All labs normal, post-event prolactin not elevated

optimal_actions.action: Explain the diagnosis to the patient in a clear, supportive, and non-dismissive manner, emphasizing that FND is a real neurological condition with effective treatments
Refer to psychiatry for evaluation and management of comorbid anxiety, depression, and possible PTSD
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identification and treatment of psychological comorbidities

optimal_actions.action: Refer to cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) with a therapist experienced in FND/PNES
Refer to physiotherapy with expertise in functional motor disorders for movement retraining
Consider optimizing antidepressant therapy for comorbid mood and anxiety disorders
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sertraline dose increase or switch to SNRI may be beneficial

optimal_actions.action: Order EMG/NCS if clinical concern for peripheral neuropathy persists
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EMG/NCS ruling out neuromuscular etiology

critical_actions: Identify positive functional neurological signs on examination (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, inconsistency)
Capture habitual events on video-EEG to confirm absence of epileptiform correlate
Exclude structural brain pathology with MRI
Diagnose FND based on positive clinical signs, NOT merely by exclusion of other diseases
Communicate the diagnosis supportively and validate that symptoms are real
Refer for appropriate psychological and rehabilitative treatment
contraindicated_actions: Initiating anti-seizure medications (carbamazepine, levetiracetam, valproate, etc.) for non-epileptic events
Telling the patient symptoms are 'all in her head' or 'not real'
Dismissing the patient or attributing symptoms to malingering without evidence
Ordering excessive invasive testing (e.g., cerebral angiography, brain biopsy) when positive functional signs are present and workup is negative
Starting opioids or benzodiazepines for symptom management
Prolonged immobilization or wheelchair prescription without rehabilitation plan
key_reasoning_points: FND is diagnosed by POSITIVE clinical signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, inconsistency, incongruity) — not by exclusion
Hoover sign is a validated, specific sign for functional leg weakness: involuntary hip extension occurs during contralateral hip flexion testing, demonstrating preserved motor pathways
Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) are diagnosed by capturing events on video-EEG showing no epileptiform correlate
Key PNES features include: arrhythmic movements, forced eye closure, retained awareness, waxing and waning intensity, side-to-side head movements, no post-ictal confusion, normal post-event prolactin
Non-dermatomal sensory loss with exact midline splitting is a positive functional sign — organic lesions do not produce exact midline splits due to bilateral innervation of midline structures
Normal MRI and labs help exclude organic mimics but do NOT alone make the diagnosis — positive signs are required
Psychiatric comorbidities (anxiety, depression, PTSD) are common in FND and are important treatment targets, but FND is NOT a psychiatric diagnosis — it is a neurological disorder
Psychosocial stressors (workplace conflict, financial stress, MVA) are predisposing and precipitating factors but are not required for diagnosis
Early diagnosis with supportive communication and multidisciplinary treatment (CBT, physiotherapy, psychiatry) leads to better outcomes

teaching_points: FND is diagnosed by positive signs, not exclusion
Video-EEG is the gold standard for diagnosing PNES
Hoover sign is the most validated positive sign for functional leg weakness
Anti-seizure medications should NOT be started for PNES
